examples.title: Modus Ponens Implementation
examples.description: Algorithm to check and apply Modus Ponens
examples.explanation: This function checks if the premises match Modus Ponens pattern (P→Q)∧P and returns Q if valid.

examples.title: Truth Table Validity Checker
examples.description: Algorithm to check argument validity using truth tables
examples.code:
FUNCTION CheckValidity(premises[], conclusion):
 propositions ← ExtractUniqueProps(premises, conclusion)
 n ← propositions.length
 
 FOR each row from 0 to (2^n - 1):
 truthValues ← GetBinaryRepresentation(row, n)
 AssignTruthValues(prop, truthValues)
 
 allPremisesTrue ← TRUE
 FOR each premise in premises:
 IF Evaluate(premise) = FALSE:
 allPremisesTrue ← FALSE
 BREAK
 
 IF allPremisesTrue AND Evaluate(conclusion) = FALSE:
 RETURN FALSE // Invalid: T premises, F conclusion
 
 RETURN TRUE // Valid

// Example: P→Q, Q→R ⊢ P→R returns TRUE
examples.explanation: Exhaustively checks all possible truth assignments. If any row has all TRUE premises but FALSE conclusion, argument is invalid. Otherwise valid.

examples.title: Natural Deduction Proof for P→R from P→Q, Q→R
examples.description: Step-by-step proof using hypothetical syllogism and modus ponens
examples.code:
// Goal: Prove P→R from premises: 1. P→Q, 2. Q→R

1. ASSUME P // Assumption for →-Introduction
2. P→Q // Premise
3. Q→R // Premise
4. Q // →-Elimination (Modus Ponens) from 2,3
5. R // →-Elimination (Modus Ponens) from 1,4
6. P→R // →-Introduction, discharging assumption 1

// Formal: From 1-5, discharge assumption to get P→R
examples.explanation: Natural deduction uses assumption and discharge. We assume P temporarily, derive R, then discharge to get P→R. This is Hypothetical Syllogism.

examples.title: Resolution Algorithm
examples.description: Resolution refutation for automated theorem proving
examples.code:
FUNCTION Resolution(clauses[]):
 newClauses ← []
 
 FOR i from 0 to clauses.length-1:
 FOR j from i+1 to clauses.length-1:
 // Find complementary literals
 complement ← FindComplement(clauses[i], clauses[j])
 IF complement exists:
 resolvent ← Resolve(clauses[i], clauses[j], complement)
 IF resolvent is empty set:
 RETURN FALSE // Proof by contradiction
 Add resolvent to newClauses
 
 IF no new clauses can be derived:
 RETURN TRUE // Cannot prove
 ELSE
 RETURN Resolution(newClauses)

// Example: {P∨Q, ¬P∨R} resolves to Q∨R
examples.explanation: Resolution recursively resolves pairs of clauses containing complementary literals until empty clause (contradiction) is derived or no more resolutions possible.
